summary:
The available literature does not provide sufficient evidence linking specific "strategy training" protocols to reduced age-related working memory deficits, due to lack of definitional clarity and measured outcomes focused solely on working memory. While related cognitive interventions show mild benefits, they are insufficiently detailed or validated for concluding effectiveness against working memory decline.

The available literature does not clearly establish that strategy training reduces age-related working memory deficits due to vague terminology and missing focus on core working memory measures. Evidence supports general cognitive gains without demonstrating consistent or significant improvements in actual working memory performance across aging individuals.  
\boxed{C}

Corrected summary:

There is inadequate evidence showing that strategy training effectively mitigates age-related working memory declines, primarily because most cited interventions use imprecise language and fail to measure critical working memory functions like those assessed in standardized tests. Consequently, despite anecdotal reports of broader cognitive benefit, no conclusive linkage exists between strategic approaches and tangible improvements in working memory capacity during aging.  
\boxed{C}
